examples.title: Indexed Addressing in Array Sum
examples.description: Calculate sum of array elements using indexed addressing mode
examples.code:
// Pseudocode for array sum using indexed addressing
FUNCTION ArraySum(array[], n)
  sum = 0
  index = 0
  baseAddr = address of array[0]
  
  WHILE index < n DO
    // Effective address = baseAddr + (index * elementSize)
    effectiveAddr = baseAddr + (index * 4)
    value = LOAD(effectiveAddr)  // Indexed addressing
    sum = sum + value
    index = index + 1
  END WHILE
  
  RETURN sum
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: This demonstrates indexed addressing where each array element is accessed by adding the index multiplied by element size to the base address. The effective address calculation: Base + (Index × Element_Size).

examples.title: RISC-Style Load-Store Operations
examples.description: Demonstrates load-store architecture typical of RISC processors
examples.code:
# RISC-style Load-Store Architecture Simulation
# Only LOAD and STORE can access memory

# Register file simulation
registers = [0] * 8  # 8 general-purpose registers
memory = [0] * 100   # Simple memory simulation

def LOAD(register_num, address):
    """Load from memory to register (LOAD instruction)"""
    registers[register_num] = memory[address]
    return registers[register_num]

def STORE(register_num, address):
    """Store from register to memory (STORE instruction)"""
    memory[address] = registers[register_num]

def ADD(dest_reg, src_reg1, src_reg2):
    """Register-to-register ADD (only operates on registers)"""
    registers[dest_reg] = registers[src_reg1] + registers[src_reg2]

def SUB(dest_reg, src_reg1, src_reg2):
    """Register-to-register SUB"""
    registers[dest_reg] = registers[src_reg1] - registers[src_reg2]

# Example: result = a[0] + a[1] using RISC-style operations
memory[10] = 5   # a[0] in memory
memory[14] = 10  # a[1] in memory

# LOAD from memory to registers
LOAD(1, 10)     # R1 = a[0]
LOAD(2, 14)     # R2 = a[1]

# ADD operates only on registers
ADD(3, 1, 2)    # R3 = R1 + R2

# STORE result back to memory
STORE(3, 20)    # result = R3

print(f"Result: {registers[3]}")  # Output: 15
examples.explanation: This demonstrates RISC's load-store architecture: memory is accessed only via LOAD and STORE instructions. All arithmetic (ADD, SUB) operates strictly between registers. This separation simplifies the instruction pipeline and enables single-cycle execution.

examples.title: CISC-Style Memory-to-Memory Operation
examples.description: Demonstrates complex instruction that performs memory-to-memory operation
examples.explanation: This shows CISC's ability to perform complex memory-to-memory operations in a single instruction. While more powerful per instruction, these complex operations typically take multiple clock cycles. Modern CISC processors internally translate these to simpler micro-operations similar to RISC.

examples.title: MIPS Addressing Modes Example
examples.description: MIPS (RISC) assembly demonstrating various addressing modes
examples.code:
# MIPS Addressing Modes Demonstration
# MIPS is a classic RISC architecture

# Immediate Addressing:
# Operand is part of instruction
addi $t0, $zero, 100     # $t0 = 0 + 100 (immediate value)
li    $t1, 50            # $t1 = 50 (load immediate)

# Register Addressing:
# Operand in register
add  $t2, $t0, $t1       # $t2 = $t0 + $t1 (register operands)

# Base-Register (Displacement) Addressing:
# Effective address = Base Register + Offset
lw   $t3, 0($t0)         # Load: $t3 = Memory[$t0 + 0]
sw   $t4, 100($sp)       # Store: Memory[$sp + 100] = $t4

# PC-Relative Addressing:
# Used in branch instructions (offset from PC)
beq  $t0, $t1, LOOP      # Branch if $t0 == $t1
                        # Target = PC + (offset * 4)
j    EXIT               # Jump: Target = PC[31:28] || address * 4

# Pseudodirect Addressing (Jump):
# Upper 4 bits of PC concatenated with 26-bit address
j    NEXT_LABEL         # Jump to label

# Register Indirect Addressing (using $t0 as pointer):
lw   $t5, 0($t0)         # Load word at address in $t0

# MIPS does NOT support:
# - Direct/Absolute addressing (full address in instruction)
# - Memory-to-memory operations
# This is typical of RISC - simple addressing modes only
examples.explanation: MIPS demonstrates RISC addressing characteristics: Immediate (addi, li), Register (add), Base-Register/Displacement (lw, sw with offset), and PC-relative (beq). Note the absence of direct addressing and memory-to-memory operations - all typical RISC limitations.
